title: Remapping Sovereignty: decolonization and self-determination in North American Indigenous political thought [Book Review]
author: Bruyneel

opening_move.description: The review opens with a sharp critique of political theory as 'disavowal' — attending to Indigenous concerns mainly as a vehicle for settler self-reflection rather than for genuine engagement with Indigenous political demands.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Political theory must move beyond 'colonial condescension' and engage seriously with Indigenous political thought on its own terms

theoretical_positioning.description: The review endorses Temin's recovery of Indigenous political thought but raises an immanent critique about the scope of 'remapping' — pressing on what it means to take Indigenous self-determination seriously in practice.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of Indigenous political thinkers and Temin's analysis; decolonial critique
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review genre with strong reviewer intervention. The reviewer's concept of 'political theory as disavowal' is the independent theoretical contribution.

case_type.case_name: North American Indigenous decolonisation movements; Temin, Remapping Sovereignty (2023)

central: Indigenous political thinkers (Coulthard, Simpson, et al. implied)

confidence_notes: Book review genre with strong immanent critique. The reviewer's independent concept ('political theory as disavowal') is the substantive contribution.
